request.question: Where is the official residence of the head of state of Germany?

title: Genadendal Residence
Government House, Jersey
Eastcliff (mansion)
Government House, Bermuda
President of Germany
State House (Kenya)
Politics of Berlin
Palais Schaumburg
Presidential palace
Hammerschmidt Villa
sentences: Genadendal Residence ] is the official residence of the President of South Africa when in office at Tuynhuys, Cape Town.
Genadendal is situated in Cape Town, Western Cape, South Africa.
It has been the official residence since 1994 when Nelson Mandela took up residence here rather than neighbouring Groote Schuur.
The building, formerly known as Westbrook, is named after the town of Genadendal – itself an Afrikaans word meaning "Valley of Mercy".
Government House is the official residence of the Lieutenant Governor of Jersey.
The building is situated in the parish of St Saviour in Jersey.
It is also used for ceremonial functions, receptions and meetings with visiting foreign dignitaries and heads of state.
It is also the official residence of the Duke of Normandy (currently Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom) as head of state when staying in Jersey.
Eastcliff is a 20-room house overlooking the Mississippi River in St. Paul, Minnesota, which serves as the official residence of the president of the University of Minnesota system.

It was first built in 1922 by local lumber magnate Edward Brooks Sr. and donated to the university by the Brooks family in 1958, beginning its service as the president's official residence in 1960 when O. Meredith Wilson took the position.
In 2000, the home was added to the National Register of Historic Places.
Today, it is the most-visited public residence in the state (outpacing the Minnesota Governor's Residence), often hosting events five days a week.
6,800 people were recorded attending 159 events in 2005.
Government House is the official residence of the Governor of Bermuda.
It is located on Langton Hill, overlooking the North Shore in Hamilton, Pembroke.
Government House is also the official residence of the Bermudian head of state (currently Queen Elizabeth II) when staying in Bermuda.
The President of Germany, officially the President of the Federal Republic of Germany (German: "Bundespräsident der Bundesrepublik Deutschland" ), is the head of state of Germany.
State House is the official residence of the President of Kenya.
It was the residence of the Prime Minister of Kenya from independence until Kenya (12 Dec 1963) transformed into a republic (12 Dec 1964).
As the Prime Minister's position was abolished, it has been the official residence of the president since then.
Berlin is a city-state and the capital of the Federal Republic of Germany.
The President of Germany, whose functions are primarily ceremonial under the German constitution, has his official residence in Schloss Bellevue.
Berlin is the seat of the German executive, housed in the Chancellery (the "Bundeskanzleramt").
Facing the Chancellery is the Bundestag, the German Parliament, housed in the renovated Reichstag building since the government's move to Berlin in 1998.
The Bundesrat ("federal council", functioning as an upper house) represents the Federal States ("Bundesländer") of Germany and has its seat at the former Prussian House of Lords.

Palais Schaumburg is a neoclassical-style building in Bonn, Germany, which served as the primary official seat of the German Federal Chancellery and the primary official residence of the Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1949 until 1999.
As the headquarters of the Federal Chancellery, it was simply known as "the House of the Federal Chancellor" (German: "Haus des Bundeskanzlers" ).
Since 1999, Palais Schaumburg has served as the secondary official seat of the German Federal Chancellery and the secondary official residence of the Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany.
A presidential palace is the official residence of the president in some countries.
However, some countries do not call the official residence of their head of state a "palace" or use the building only as a workplace separate from the president's actual home.
Some presidential palaces were once the official residences to monarchs in former monarchies that were preserved during those states' transition into republics.
The Cumhurbaşkanlığı Sarayı in the Turkey is the world's largest Presidential Palace, four times that of Versailles.

Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994.
President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994.
Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President.
In German, the Villa is also called the "White House of Bonn", because of its vague resemblance to the official residence of the President of the United States.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Where is the official residence of the head of state of Germany?
hop1_docs: Official residence | An official residence is the residence at which a nation's head of state, head of government, governor or other senior figure officially resides.  It may or may not be the same location where the individual conducts work-related functions or lives.

Royal Palace of Brussels | The Royal Palace of Brussels (Dutch: "Koninklijk Paleis van Brussel" ] , French: "Palais Royal de Bruxelles" , German: "Königlicher Palast von Brüssel" ) is the official palace of the King and Queen of the Belgians in the centre of the nation's capital Brussels.  However it is not used as a royal residence, as the king and his family live in the Royal Palace of Laeken on the outskirts of Brussels.  The website of the Belgian Monarchy describes the function of the palace as follows: ""The Palace is where His Majesty the King exercises his prerogatives as Head of State, grants audiences and deals with affairs of state.  Apart from the offices of the King and the Queen, the Royal Palace houses the services of the Grand Marshal of the Court, the King's Head of Cabinet, the Head of the King's Military Household and the Intendant of the King's Civil List.  The Palace also includes the State Rooms where large receptions are held, as well as the apartments provided for foreign Heads of State during official visits.""

Government House, Jersey | Government House is the official residence of the Lieutenant Governor of Jersey.  The building is situated in the parish of St Saviour in Jersey.  It is also used for ceremonial functions, receptions and meetings with visiting foreign dignitaries and heads of state.  It is also the official residence of the Duke of Normandy (currently Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom) as head of state when staying in Jersey.

Presidential palace | A presidential palace is the official residence of the president in some countries.  However, some countries do not call the official residence of their head of state a "palace" or use the building only as a workplace separate from the president's actual home.  Some presidential palaces were once the official residences to monarchs in former monarchies that were preserved during those states' transition into republics.  The Cumhurbaşkanlığı Sarayı in the Turkey is the world's largest Presidential Palace, four times that of Versailles.

Hammerschmidt Villa | Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994.  President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994.  Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President.  In German, the Villa is also called the "White House of Bonn", because of its vague resemblance to the official residence of the President of the United States.
The Residency, Penang | The Residency in George Town, Penang, now formally named Seri Mutiara, is the official residence of the "Yang di-Pertua Negeri" (The Head of The State) of Penang.

Grand Ducal Palace, Luxembourg | The Grand Ducal Palace (Luxembourgish: "Groussherzogleche Palais" , French: "Palais grand-ducal" , German: "Großherzogliches Palais" ) is a palace in Luxembourg City, in southern Luxembourg.  It is the official residence of the Grand Duke of Luxembourg, and where he performs most of his duties as head of state of the Grand Duchy.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Official residence — [1]: "residence at which a nation's head of state"
- Royal Palace of Brussels — [2]: "is the official palace of the King and Queen"
- Royal Palace of Laeken — [2]: "live in the Royal Palace of Laeken"
- Government House, Jersey — [3]: "official residence of the Lieutenant Governor of Jersey."
- Presidential palace — [4]: "official residence of the president in some countries."
- Cumhurbaşkanlığı Sarayı — [4]: "Cumhurbaşkanlığı Sarayı in the Turkey"
- Hammerschmidt Villa — [5]: "located in the German city of Bonn"
- Hammerschmidt Villa (dates) — [5]: "from 1950 until 1994."
- Bellevue Palace — [5]: "made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994."
- The Residency, Penang — [6]: "is the official residence of the 'Yang di-Pertua Negeri'"
- Grand Ducal Palace, Luxembourg — [7]: "is the official residence of the Grand Duke of Luxembourg"

Ambiguities:
- "official residence of the head of state of Germany" — historical overlap: Hammerschmidt Villa (Bonn) "from 1950 until 1994." vs Bellevue Palace (Berlin) "made Bellevue Palace in Berlin ... in 1994." The conflict is temporal (pre-1994 vs post-1994).

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages explicitly state Bellevue Palace in Berlin was made the President's "primary official seat and residence in 1994." This supplies the current official residence of Germany's head of state (the President) and resolves the temporal ambiguity. No additional essential fact is required to answer the question.

Retrieval direction:
- For direct confirmation, fetch page: "Bellevue Palace" (page on Bellevue Palace, Berlin).
- Fetch page: "President of Germany" (to confirm "head of state" title and official residence).
- (Optional) Fetch page: "Hammerschmidt Villa" (to confirm historical seat "from 1950 until 1994").
Final conclusion: The official residence of the head of state of Germany is Bellevue Palace in Berlin.
hop2_docs: Berlin Bellevue station | Bellevue is a railway station on the Berlin Stadtbahn in the Hansaviertel district of Berlin, Germany.  It is located on the elevated Stadtbahn line and served by Berlin S-Bahn trains.  The station is named after nearby Bellevue Palace, the residence of the President of Germany.

Bellevue Palace (Germany) | Bellevue Palace (German: "Schloss Bellevue" ), located in Berlin's Tiergarten district, has been the official residence of the President of Germany since 1994.  It is situated on the banks of the Spree river, near the Berlin Victory Column, along the northern edge of the Großer Tiergarten park.  Its namethe French for "beautiful view"derives from its scenic prospect over the Spree's course.

Hammerschmidt Villa | Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994.  President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994.  Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President.  In German, the Villa is also called the "White House of Bonn", because of its vague resemblance to the official residence of the President of the United States.

Bellevue Palace, Kassel | Bellevue Palace (German: Palais Bellevue or Schloss Bellevue) in Kassel was built in 1714 for Charles I, Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel.  Originally the building served as an Observatory.  It became a residence, and then part of Bellevue Castle, which was later destroyed.  Today the building houses a museum devoted to the Brothers Grimm.

Hotel Bellevue Palace | The Bellevue Palace is a five-star luxury hotel located in the Old City of Bern, Switzerland.  Owned by the Swiss Confederation, it is the state's guesthouse for visiting heads of state and government, and is host to dozens of members of parliament during the weeks the assembly is in session.

Politics of Berlin | Berlin is a city-state and the capital of the Federal Republic of Germany.  The President of Germany, whose functions are primarily ceremonial under the German constitution, has his official residence in Schloss Bellevue.  Berlin is the seat of the German executive, housed in the Chancellery (the "Bundeskanzleramt").  Facing the Chancellery is the Bundestag, the German Parliament, housed in the renovated Reichstag building since the government's move to Berlin in 1998.  The Bundesrat ("federal council", functioning as an upper house) represents the Federal States ("Bundesländer") of Germany and has its seat at the former Prussian House of Lords.

Capital of Germany | The capital of Germany is the city state of Berlin.  It is the seat of the President of Germany, whose official residence is Schloss Bellevue.  The Bundesrat ("federal council") is the representation of the Federal States ("Bundesländer") of Germany and has its seat at the former Prussian Herrenhaus (House of Lords).  Though most of the ministries are seated in Berlin, some of them, as well as some minor departments, are seated in Bonn, the former capital of West Germany.
response.summary_2: The official residence of the head of state of Germany is Bellevue Palace (Schloss Bellevue) in Berlin.
